html:
[1053]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大抵 - Generally, usually; a common or typical situation.
  • 士大夫之習氣 - The habits or customs of scholars and officials.
  • 類以不談人過為君子 - Tends to regard not talking about others' faults as a mark of a gentleman.
  • 不計其人之親疏，事之利害 - Does not consider the closeness or distance of the relationship, or the benefits or harms involved.
  • 余常见胡牧亭为群仆剥削，至衣食不给 - I often see Hu Mutting being exploited by his servants, to the point where he cannot provide for his clothing and food.
  • 同年朱学士竹君，奋然代为驱逐 - The same year's scholar-official Zhu Zhujun, bravely drove them away.
  • 生计乃稍苏 - His livelihood was somewhat revived.
  • 孀妾孤儿 - Widowed wife and orphaned children.
  • 为其婿所凌逼 - Being bullied by her son-in-law.
  • 同年曹宗丞慕堂亦奋然鸠率旧好，代为驱逐 - The same year's official Cao Mutang also bravely gathered old friends and drove them away.
  • 其子乃得以自存 - His son was thus able to survive.
  • 一时清议 - At that time, public opinion.
  • 称古道者百不一二 - Those who praised ancient virtues were few and far between.
  • 称多事者十恒八九 - Those who called it meddling were about eight or nine out of ten.
  • 崔总宪应阶娶孙妇，赁彩轿亲迎 - Cui Zongxian Yingjie married his granddaughter, renting a decorated sedan chair for the wedding.
  • 其家奴互相钩贯 - His household slaves conspired together.
  • 非三百金不能得 - Without three hundred taels of silver, it could not be obtained.
  • 众喙一音 - All voices were in unison.
  • 至前期一两日，价更倍昂 - By the day or two before the event, the price doubled.
  • 崔公恚愤 - Cui Gong was filled with anger.
  • 自求朋友代赁 - He sought friends to rent on his behalf.
  • 朋友皆避怨不肯应 - Friends all avoided resentment and did not respond.
  • 甚有谓彩轿无定价，贫富贵贱各随其人为消长，非他人所可代赁，以巧为调停者 - Some even said that the decorated sedan chair had no fixed price, and the cost varied according to the person's wealth, and it was not something others could rent on their behalf, using cleverness to mediate.
  • 不得已以己所乘轿，结皕缯用之 - Unable to do otherwise, he used his own sedan chair, decorated with two hundred pieces of silk.
  • 坐视非理者 - Those who stood by and watched without intervening.
  • 善体下情者 - Those who understood the lower people's feelings.
  • 彼一是非，此一是非 - This is right, that is wrong; a matter of perspective.
  • 将乌乎质之哉 - How can one judge between them?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 士大夫 - Scholars and officials, a class of educated and influential people in traditional Chinese society.
  • 君子 - A person of high moral character, often used to describe a gentleman.
  • 清议 - Public opinion, especially in the context of moral and ethical judgments.
  • 崔总宪 - Cui Zongxian, a high-ranking official during the Qing dynasty.
  • 彩轿 - Decorated sedan chair, a traditional Chinese wedding vehicle.

Proper Nouns

  • 胡牧亭 - Hu Mutting, a specific individual mentioned in the text.
  • 朱学士竹君 - Zhu Zhujun, a scholar-official mentioned in the text.
  • 陈裕斋 - Chen Yuzhai, a specific individual mentioned in the text.
  • 曹宗丞慕堂 - Cao Mutang, an official mentioned in the text.
  • 崔总宪应阶 - Cui Zongxian Yingjie, a high-ranking official mentioned in the text.

[1054]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 長揖 - A formal bow, often used in ancient China as a sign of respect.
  • 品秩相等 - Of equal rank, referring to the same level of official position.
  • 庭參禮 - Court ceremony, a formal ritual performed by officials.
  • 怫然 - Angry or indignant, expressing a strong negative reaction.
  • 善辩者无如何 - Even the best debaters cannot do anything about it, suggesting that the situation is beyond argument.
  • 以不治治之 - To treat it with non-treatment, a paradoxical phrase suggesting a non-interventionist approach.
  • 于事无害 - No harm to the matter, indicating that the approach is harmless.
  • 喧诟至夜半 - Shouting and cursing until midnight, describing a prolonged argument.
  • 破额诣讼庭 - Went to the court with broken foreheads, a metaphor for seeking justice after a conflict.
  • 天下本无事，庸人自扰之 - There is no inherent trouble in the world; it is the mediocre who create it, a proverb suggesting that problems are often self-inflicted.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 朱青雷 - Zhu Qinglei, a historical figure.
  • 椒山祠 - Jiashan Temple, a temple dedicated to a historical figure.
  • 楊公 - Yang Gong, a title for a historical figure.
  • 忠臣 - Loyal minister, a term used to describe someone who is loyal to the emperor.
  • 奸臣 - Traitorous minister, a term used to describe someone who is disloyal to the emperor.
  • 聂松岩 - Nie Songyan, a historical figure.
  • 治磨者 - Stone mason, a person who works with stone.
  • 石工 - Stone worker, another term for a person who works with stone.
  • 篆刻 - Seal carving, an art form involving the creation of seals.
  • 安邱 - Anqiu, a place name.
  • 卯君 - Mao Jun, a person's name.
  • 同岁入泮 - Enrolled in the same year, referring to the same year of entering the school.
  • 不第 - Failed the examination, indicating failure in the imperial examinations.
  • 田白岩 - Tian Baiyan, a historical figure.
  • 詈紫阳 - Cursing Ziyang, a reference to a specific person or place.
  • 詈象山 - Cursing Xiangshan, another reference to a specific person or place.
  • 异端害正 - Heterodoxies harm the orthodox, a term used to describe heretical beliefs.

Proper Nouns

  • 朱青雷 - Zhu Qinglei, a historical figure.
  • 椒山祠 - Jiashan Temple, a temple dedicated to a historical figure.
  • 楊公 - Yang Gong, a title for a historical figure.
  • 員外郎 - A title of a junior official in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 忠臣 - Loyal minister, a term used to describe someone who is loyal to the emperor.
  • 奸臣 - Traitorous minister, a term used to describe someone who is disloyal to the emperor.
  • 聂松岩 - Nie Songyan, a historical figure.
  • 治磨者 - Stone mason, a person who works with stone.
  • 石工 - Stone worker, another term for a person who works with stone.
  • 篆刻 - Seal carving, an art form involving the creation of seals.
  • 安邱 - Anqiu, a place name.
  • 卯君 - Mao Jun, a person's name.
  • 同岁入泮 - Enrolled in the same year, referring to the same year of entering the school.
  • 不第 - Failed the examination, indicating failure in the imperial examinations.
  • 田白岩 - Tian Baiyan, a historical figure.
  • 詈紫阳 - Cursing Ziyang, a reference to a specific person or place.
  • 詈象山 - Cursing Xiangshan, another reference to a specific person or place.

[1055]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蓄鸡至多 - Literally 'keep many chickens,' meaning she has a large number of chickens.
  • 卖其卵 - Means 'sell their eggs,' referring to the practice of selling eggs rather than chickens.
  • 买鸡充馔者 - Refers to people who buy chickens for food, with '充馔' meaning 'for cooking or meals.'
  • 虽十倍其价不肯售 - Means 'even if the price is ten times higher, she would not sell,' indicating her reluctance to sell chickens.
  • 依山麓 - Means 'relying on the foothills,' describing the location of her residence.
  • 日久滋衍 - Means 'over time, they multiply,' referring to the increasing number of chickens.
  • 殆以谷量 - Means 'almost counted by the grain,' suggesting the chickens are so numerous that they can be measured in large quantities.
  • 将曙时 - Means 'just before dawn,' referring to the time when the chickens start crowing.
  • 唱声竞作 - Means 'their crowing sounds compete,' describing the loud and simultaneous crowing of the chickens.
  • 传呼之相应也 - Means 'like a call and response,' comparing the chickens' crowing to a coordinated sound.
  • 会刈麦暴于门外 - Means 'just as the wheat was being cut and laid out to dry outside,' describing a specific event.
  • 群鸡忽千百齐至 - Means 'a flock of chickens suddenly gathered in the hundreds,' indicating an unexpected and large gathering.
  • 围绕啄食 - Means 'gathered around and pecking at the food,' describing the chickens' behavior.
  • 持杖驱之不开 - Means 'she tried to drive them away with a stick but they wouldn’t move,' indicating the difficulty in dispersing the chickens.
  • 遍呼男女交手扑击 - Means 'she called out to men and women to help and they beat them with their hands,' describing the collective effort to disperse the chickens.
  • 东散西聚 - Means 'they scattered in all directions,' describing the chaotic movement of the chickens.
  • 莫可如何 - Means 'nothing could be done,' indicating the inability to control the situation.
  • 方喧呶间 - Means 'just as the commotion was happening,' describing the ongoing chaos.
  • 住屋五楹 - Means 'the five-room house,' referring to the structure that collapsed.
  • 訇然摧圮 - Means 'collapsed with a loud noise,' describing the sudden and dramatic collapse.
  • 鸡乃俱惊飞入山去 - Means 'the chickens then all flew into the mountains in fright,' describing the chickens' reaction to the collapse.
  • 鹤知夜半，鸡知将旦 - Means 'cranes know midnight, chickens know dawn,' referring to the natural instincts of animals.
  • 气之相感而精神动焉 - Means 'the interaction of their energies causes their spirits to move,' describing the spiritual connection between animals and their environment.
  • 非其能自知时也 - Means 'it is not that they know time themselves,' indicating that animals do not consciously understand time.
  • 故邵子曰：禽鸟得气之先 - Means 'Therefore, Master Shao said: birds and animals sense the energy first,' referring to the philosopher Shao Yong’s observation.
  • 至万物成毁之数 - Means 'as for the fate of all things coming and going,' referring to the natural cycles of creation and destruction.
  • 断非禽鸟所先知 - Means 'it is certainly not something birds and animals know beforehand,' indicating that animals do not foresee natural events.
  • 何以聚族而来，脱主人于厄乎 - Means 'how did they gather in a group and save their master from danger?' questioning the chickens' behavior.
  • 此必有凭之者矣 - Means 'this must be something they rely on,' suggesting that there is a deeper reason or spiritual force behind the chickens' actions.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 昌平 - A district in modern-day Beijing, historically significant for its role in Chinese history.
  • 宣室志 - A collection of stories and anecdotes, often containing supernatural or mystical elements.
  • 李甲家鼠报恩事 - A specific story from the *Xuan Shi* about a mouse repaying a favor, often used to illustrate themes of gratitude and supernatural intervention.
  • 邵子 - Refers to Shao Yong (1011–1077), a prominent Chinese philosopher and writer of the Song Dynasty, known for his works on cosmology and divination.

Proper Nouns

  • 昌平 - A place name, likely referring to a district in modern-day Beijing.
  • 宣室志 - A collection of stories and anecdotes, often containing supernatural or mystical elements.
  • 李甲家鼠报恩事 - A specific story from the *Xuan Shi* about a mouse repaying a favor.
  • 邵子 - Refers to Shao Yong, a prominent Chinese philosopher and writer of the Song Dynasty.

[1056]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 从侄 - Refers to a cousin who is younger than the speaker.
  • 捕狐为业 - Means 'to make a living by catching foxes.'
  • 相其穴可容人 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'to examine the den and see if it could hold a person.'
  • 伏穴内 - Means 'to hide inside the den.'
  • 匿冢畔丛薄中 - Means 'to conceal oneself in the dense vegetation near the grave.'
  • 待狐归穴 - Means 'to wait for the fox to return to its den.'
  • 御其出路 - Means 'to block its exit.'
  • 禽絷之 - Means 'to catch and restrain it.'
  • 寂无音响 - Means 'silent and without any sound.'
  • 呼良久不应 - Means 'called out for a long time but received no response.'
  • 试出寻之 - Means 'tried to go out and look for it.'
  • 二墓碑横压穴口 - Means 'two tombstones were lying across the entrance of the den.'
  • 仅隙光一线，阔寸许 - Means 'only a narrow slit of light, about an inch wide.'
  • 重不可举 - Means 'too heavy to lift.'
  • 乃知为甲所卖 - Means 'then realized he had been betrayed by A.'
  • 幽闭一昼夜 - Means 'imprisoned for a day and night.'
  • 疑甲谋杀 - Means 'suspected A of attempted murder.'
  • 报讼官 - Means 'to report to the authorities.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 捕狐为业 - A specific occupation in ancient China, reflecting the economic activities and survival strategies of rural communities.
  • 墓碑 - Tombstones were common in ancient China and often served as markers for graves.
  • 幽闭 - A term used in ancient Chinese legal and social contexts to describe imprisonment or confinement.
  • 报讼官 - Reporting to the authorities was a standard procedure for dealing with disputes and crimes in ancient Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 汝夔 - The name of the narrator's cousin.
  • 甲 - The first character's name.
  • 乙 - The second character's name.

[1057]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至半途乃见 - Literal meaning 'until halfway, then see,' indicating a sudden discovery or encounter.
  • 反缚 - Means 'bound with hands behind the back,' a common form of restraint.
  • 唾詈 - Means 'spit at and curse,' a vivid description of public humiliation.
  • 鞭朴之 - Means 'whip and beat,' describing physical punishment.
  • 盖甲赴约时 - Means 'when Jia was going to meet someone,' introducing the context of the encounter.
  • 路遇妇相调谑 - Means 'met a woman on the road who teased him,' describing an interaction.
  • 因私狎于秫丛 - Means 'and secretly had intimate relations in a millet thicket,' indicating a clandestine affair.
  • 各解衣置地 - Means 'each undressed and placed their clothes on the ground,' describing the preparation for intimacy.
  • 甫脱手 - Means 'just as they were about to let go,' indicating the moment of the woman's escape.
  • 掣其衣走 - Means 'she grabbed her clothes and ran,' describing the woman's sudden departure.
  • 莫知所向 - Means 'no one knew where she went,' indicating the uncertainty of her whereabouts.
  • 狼狈潜归 - Means 'returned in a disheveled state,' describing Jia's hurried retreat.
  • 未至家遇明火持械者 - Means 'before reaching home, he met a group of armed robbers,' introducing a new threat.
  • 见之呼曰：奴在此 - Means 'they saw him and shouted, 'The slave is here!'' describing the robbers' accusation.
  • 忽见甲解衣就同卧 - Means 'they suddenly saw Jia undressing and lying down,' describing the robbers' mistaken perception.
  • 惊唤众起 - Means 'they were startled and called everyone to wake up,' indicating the reaction to the perceived threat.
  • 已弃衣矴墙遁 - Means 'he had already thrown off his clothes and fled over the wall,' describing Jia's escape.
  • 方其里党追捕也 - Means 'just as the villagers were pursuing him,' indicating the ongoing pursuit.
  • 甲无以自白 - Means 'Jia had no way to clear his name,' describing his helplessness.
  • 惟呼天而已 - Means 'he could only cry out to heaven,' indicating his despair.
  • 乙述昨事 - Means 'Yi recounted the events of yesterday,' introducing a new narrator.
  • 乃知皆为狐所卖 - Means 'then realized that they had all been tricked by a fox,' indicating the supernatural explanation.
  • 然伺其穴而掩袭 - Means 'but waited for its den and ambushed it,' describing a planned counterattack.
  • 此戕杀之仇也 - Means 'this is a grudge for killing,' indicating the severity of the revenge.
  • 以游戏报之 - Means 'to repay it with a game,' describing the method of revenge.
  • 一闭使不出而留隙使不死 - Means 'one to seal it so it cannot escape and leave a gap so it does not die,' indicating the strategy of the revenge.
  • 一褫其衣使受缚无辩 - Means 'one to strip its clothes so it is bound and cannot defend itself,' describing the method of binding.
  • 人觉即遁 - Means 'if anyone notices, it will flee,' indicating the fox's cunning.
  • 使其罪亦不至死 - Means 'even if its crime does not warrant death,' indicating the leniency of the revenge.
  • 犹可谓善留余地矣 - Means 'it can still be said to have left room for leniency,' indicating the moral judgment of the revenge.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐 - Refers to a fox, often associated with trickery and cunning in Chinese folklore.
  • 里党 - Refers to the local community or village, indicating the social context of the story.
  • 明火持械者 - Refers to armed robbers, indicating a dangerous encounter.
  • 解衣就同卧 - Refers to undressing and lying down, indicating a mistaken perception of intimacy.
  • 弃衣矴墙遁 - Refers to throwing off clothes and fleeing over a wall, indicating a desperate escape.
  • 呼天 - Refers to crying out to heaven, indicating a plea for help or justice.
  • 自白 - Refers to clearing one's name, indicating the difficulty of proving innocence.
  • 狐所卖 - Refers to being tricked by a fox, indicating a supernatural explanation for the events.
  • 戕杀之仇 - Refers to a grudge for killing, indicating the severity of the revenge.
  • 游戏报之 - Refers to repaying with a game, indicating a strategic and cunning method of revenge.
  • 闭使不出而留隙使不死 - Refers to sealing and leaving a gap, indicating a balanced and measured approach to revenge.
  • 褫其衣使受缚无辩 - Refers to stripping clothes and binding, indicating a method of incapacitation.
  • 人觉即遁 - Refers to fleeing if noticed, indicating the fox's cunning.
  • 善留余地 - Refers to leaving room for leniency, indicating a moral judgment on the revenge.

Proper Nouns

  • 甲 - Refers to a man named Jia, the main character of the story.
  • 乙 - Refers to a man named Yi, a new narrator who recounts the events.
  • 狐 - Refers to a fox, often associated with trickery and cunning in Chinese folklore.
  • 里党 - Refers to the local community or village, indicating the social context of the story.
  • 明火持械者 - Refers to armed robbers, indicating a dangerous encounter.

[1058]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 天下有极细之事 - Refers to extremely minor or trivial matters in the world.
  • 皋陶亦不能断者 - Refers to cases that even Gao Yao (a legendary judge in ancient China) could not resolve.
  • 门人折生遇兰 - Refers to a student named Zhe Sheng Yu Lan, described as a capable official.
  • 官安定日 - Means 'when he was serving as an official in Anding.'
  • 有两家争一坟山 - Refers to a dispute between two families over a burial hill.
  • 讼四五十年 - Means 'a lawsuit lasting forty to fifty years.'
  • 阅两世矣 - Means 'spanning two generations.'
  • 其地广阔不盈亩 - Describes the land as vast but not exceeding one mu (a unit of area).
  • 中有二冢 - Refers to two graves in the middle of the land.
  • 各以为祖茔 - Means 'each family claims it as their ancestral tomb.'
  • 问邻证则万山之中，裹粮挈水乃能至 - Means 'if you ask local witnesses, they are in the midst of countless mountains, and one must carry food and water to reach them.'
  • 四无居人 - Means 'there are no residents around.'
  • 问契券则皆称前明兵燹 - Means 'if you ask about deeds, they all say that the documents were destroyed during the Ming dynasty's wars.'
  • 已不存 - Means 'no longer exist.'
  • 问地粮串票 - Refers to asking about land tax receipts.
  • 两造具在 - Means 'both parties are present.'
  • 其词皆曰 - Means 'their statements are all the same.'
  • 此地万不足耕 - Means 'this land is not suitable for farming.'
  • 无锱铢之利 - Means 'there is no profit to be gained.'
  • 而有地丁之额 - Means 'but there is a land tax quota.'
  • 所以百控不已者 - Means 'the reason they continue to appeal endlessly.'
  • 徒以祖宗丘陇 - Means 'simply because of their ancestors' graves.'
  • 不欲为他人占耳 - Means 'they do not want others to take them.'
  • 苟非先人之体魄 - Means 'if it were not the remains of their ancestors.'
  • 谁肯涉讼数十年 - Means 'who would litigate for decades.'
  • 认他人为祖宗者 - Means 'and acknowledge others as their ancestors.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 皋陶 - A legendary judge in ancient China known for his wisdom and fairness.
  • 前明兵燹 - Refers to the destruction caused by wars during the Ming dynasty.
  • 地粮串票 - Refers to land tax receipts, indicating the official recognition of land ownership.
  • 地丁之额 - Refers to the land tax quota, a form of taxation based on land ownership.

Proper Nouns

  • 皋陶 - A legendary judge in ancient China.
  • 折生遇兰 - The name of the official.
  • 前明 - Refers to the Ming dynasty.
  • 地粮串票 - Refers to land tax receipts.
  • 地丁之额 - Refers to the land tax quota.

[1059]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 或疑为谋占吉地 - Some suspect that it is a plot to seize auspicious burial grounds.
  • 秦陇素不讲此事 - Qin and Long regions have never discussed this matter before.
  • 实无此心 - They truly have no such intention.
  • 彼此不疑有此心 - They do not doubt that the other party has such an intention.
  • 四周皆石，不能再容一棺 - The surrounding area is entirely rocky, making it impossible to accommodate another coffin.
  • 如得地之后，掘而别葬 - If they were to obtain the land and then dig up and re-bury the remains.
  • 是反授不得者以间 - It would be tantamount to giving the opportunity to those who cannot have it.
  • 谁敢为之 - Who would dare to do such a thing?
  • 竟无以折服 - There is no way to convince them.
  • 又无均分理，无入官理 - There is no way to divide it equally, nor can it be resolved by involving the authorities.
  • 亦莫能判定 - It cannot be decided either.
  • 大抵每祭必斗，每斗必讼 - Generally, every time they worship, they fight, and every fight leads to a lawsuit.
  • 官惟就斗论斗，更不问其所因矣 - The officials only deal with the fighting, not asking about the cause.
  • 盍以理喻之 - Why not reason with them?
  • 尔既自以为祖墓 - You claim it as your ancestral tomb.
  • 应听尔祭 - You should be allowed to worship.
  • 其来争祭者，既愿以尔祖为祖 - Those who come to dispute the right to worship, already wish to consider your ancestors as their own.
  • 于尔祖无损，于尔亦无损也 - It does no harm to your ancestors, nor to you.
  • 听其享荐亦大佳 - Allowing them to offer sacrifices is also very good.
  • 何必拒乎 - Why reject them?
  • 亦不得已之权词 - It is also a temporary solution out of necessity.
  • 然迄不知其遵否也 - However, it is unknown whether they followed this advice.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秦陇 - Refers to the Qin and Long regions, which are areas in modern-day Gansu Province.
  • 蔡西斋 - A historical figure who served as the governor of Gansu Province.
  • 藩司 - A high-ranking official in the Qing dynasty, responsible for provincial administration.
  • 祖墓 - Refers to ancestral tombs, which are considered sacred and important in Chinese culture.
  • 享荐 - Refers to the act of offering sacrifices to ancestors.

[1061]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 官乃大沮去 - Literally 'the official then greatly became disheartened and left,' indicating a sense of profound disappointment or discouragement.
  • 其他往往多类此 - Means 'others often similarly,' suggesting a pattern or trend.
  • 乡人皆言其蠢然一物 - 'The villagers all say he is a foolish creature,' using '蠢然' to describe someone as dull or unintelligent.
  • 乃有此福，理不可明 - 'Yet he has this fortune, the reason cannot be understood,' expressing a sense of mystery or unexplained luck.
  • 扶乩召仙 - 'Calling upon spirits through divination,' a traditional Chinese practice involving a medium (乩) to communicate with the spiritual world.
  • 以此叩之 - 'To ask about this,' referring to inquiring about the matter at hand.
  • 乩判曰 - 'The divination response said,' indicating the reply received from the spiritual medium.
  • 诸公误矣 - 'You gentlemen are mistaken,' a direct correction.
  • 其福正以其蠢也 - 'His fortune is precisely because of his foolishness,' suggesting that his lack of intelligence is the cause of his good fortune.
  • 此翁过去生中，乃一村叟 - 'This old man in his past life was a village elder,' referring to a previous incarnation.
  • 其人淳淳闷闷无计较心 - 'He was simple and dull, without any calculating mind,' describing a person who is straightforward and not scheming.
  • 悠悠忽忽无得失心 - 'He was carefree and indifferent to gains and losses,' indicating a lack of concern for material outcomes.
  • 落落漠漠无爱憎心 - 'He was detached and indifferent to likes and dislikes,' showing a lack of emotional attachment.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a recurring punishment and its effects: '今每至月朔，必受铁杖三十，未知何日得脱，此累累者皆是也'.
  • This sentence describes an attempt to speak further but being dragged away by spirits: '尚欲再言，已为鬼卒曳去'.
  • This sentence explains the reason for the actions taken: '多年伉俪，未免有情，故为营斋造福耳'.
  • This sentence explains the significance of the traditional wedding ceremony: '夫同牢之礼，于情最亲，亲则非疏者所能间'.
  • This sentence explains the principle of equality in marital relationships: '敌体之义，于分本尊，尊则非卑者所能违'.
  • This sentence explains the benefits of mutual understanding and cooperation: '故二人同心，则家庭之纤微曲折，男子所不能知，与知而不能自为者，皆足以弥缝其阙'.
  • This sentence explains the consequences of favoritism and manipulation: '苟徇其私爱，意有所偏，则机械百出，亦可于耳目所不及者，无所不为'.
  • This sentence explains the significant consequences of such actions: '种种衅端，种种败坏，皆从是起，所关者大，则其罪自不得轻'.
  • This sentence explains the severity of betrayal and its consequences: '况信之者至深，托之者至重，而欺其不觉，为所欲为，在朋友犹属负心，应干神谴，则人原一体，分属三纲者，其负心之罪，不更加倍蓰乎'.
  • This sentence explains the application of strict punishment for minor offenses: '寻常细故，断以严刑，因不得谓之深文矣'.

  • The passage contains several syntactic ambiguities, including the use of ellipsis and the need to clarify the relationships between clauses. Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This phrase indicates that the young female servant was harshly interrogated and fully confessed to selling the bracelet to a street vendor.
  • This phrase indicates that despite the initial confession, the bracelet was not found, and the interrogation continued.
  • This phrase introduces an unexpected voice from above, suggesting a supernatural element.
  • This phrase is a question posed by the voice, suggesting that the bracelet might have been mistakenly placed in a lacquer box.
  • This phrase indicates that the bracelet was found as suggested, confirming the accuracy of the voice's suggestion.
  • This phrase indicates that the young female servant was severely beaten during the interrogation.
  • This phrase indicates that Liu Nishan felt deep regret and often recounted the story.
  • This phrase suggests that while such incidents happen frequently, it is not possible to have such helpful spirits everywhere.
  • This phrase indicates that Liu Nishan, after his experience, never resorted to torture in his judicial career.

  • 宋儒精而昌黎粗 - Comparative phrase meaning 'the Song scholars are refined while Han Yu is coarse,' further contrasting the refinement of Song scholars with the apparent crudeness of Han Yu.
  • 披缁之徒 - Refers to Buddhist monks, literally 'those who wear the yellow robe.'
  • 畏昌黎不畏宋儒 - Means 'fear Han Yu but not the Song scholars,' indicating a specific attitude toward Han Yu and Song scholars.
  • 衔昌黎不衔宋儒 - Means 'resent Han Yu but not the Song scholars,' indicating a specific attitude toward Han Yu and Song scholars.
  • 檀施供养之佛 - Refers to the Buddha as an object of charity and worship, literally 'the Buddha of alms and offerings.'
  • 为愚夫妇言之 - Means 'spoken to the common people,' indicating the intended audience of Han Yu's critique.
  • 明心见性之佛 - Refers to the Buddha as an object of spiritual insight, literally 'the Buddha of enlightened mind and nature.'
  • 为士大夫言之 - Means 'spoken to the literati,' indicating the intended audience of the Song scholars' critique.
  • 天下士大夫少而愚夫妇多 - Comparative phrase meaning 'there are fewer literati than common people in the world,' highlighting the demographic distribution.
  • 僧徒之所取给 - Refers to the means of support for Buddhist monks, literally 'the means by which monks sustain themselves.'
  • 资于士大夫者少，资于愚夫妇者多 - Means 'relying on the literati is less, relying on the common people is more,' indicating the sources of support for monks.
  • 使昌黎之说胜 - Means 'if Han Yu's critique prevails,' introducing a conditional scenario.
  • 香积无烟，祗园无地 - Metaphorical expressions meaning 'no offerings would be made, no monasteries would exist,' indicating the potential consequences of Han Yu's critique.
  • 虽有大善知识 - Means 'even if there are great wise teachers,' introducing a hypothetical scenario.
  • 能率恒河沙众，枵腹露宿而说法哉 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'could they lead countless followers to preach with empty stomachs and exposed to the elements?' indicating the impracticality of the scenario.
  • 此如用兵者，先断粮道，不攻而自溃也 - Military metaphor meaning 'it is like a military commander who first cuts off the enemy's supply lines, causing them to collapse without a fight,' illustrating the effectiveness of Han Yu's critique.
  • 使宋儒之说胜 - Means 'if the Song scholars' critique prevails,' introducing another conditional scenario.
  • 尔儒理如是，儒法如是 - Means 'your Confucian principles are like this, your Confucian methods are like this,' indicating the content of the Song scholars' critique.
  • 尔不必从我，我佛理如是，佛法如是 - Means 'you do not need to follow me, my Buddhist principles are like this, my Buddhist methods are like this,' indicating the content of the Song scholars' critique.
  • 各尊所闻，各行所知 - Means 'each respects what they hear, each acts according to what they know,' indicating a state of mutual respect and independence.
  • 两相枝拄，未有害也 - Means 'they support each other, causing no harm,' indicating a harmonious relationship.
  • 故不畏宋儒，亦不甚衔宋儒 - Means 'therefore, they do not fear the Song scholars, nor do they greatly resent them,' indicating a neutral attitude toward the Song scholars.
  • 唐以前之儒 - Refers to scholars before the Tang dynasty.
  • 语语有实用 - Means 'every word has practical use,' indicating the practicality of pre-Tang scholars.
  • 宋以后之儒 - Refers to scholars after the Tang dynasty.
  • 事事皆空谈 - Means 'everything is empty talk,' indicating the lack of practicality of post-Tang scholars.
  • 讲学家之辟佛 - Refers to the critique of Buddhism by scholars, literally 'the critique of Buddhism by scholars.'
  • 于释氏毫无所加损 - Means 'it does not add or subtract anything from Buddhism,' indicating the neutrality of the critique.
  • 徒喧哄耳 - Means 'it is just noise,' indicating the futility of the critique.
  • 录以为功，固为党论 - Means 'if recorded as a contribution, it is indeed a partisan view,' indicating the bias of the critique.
  • 录以为罪，亦未免重视留良耳 - Means 'if recorded as a fault, it is also a matter of valuing Han Yu too highly,' indicating the bias of the critique.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 救生不救死者 - A moral principle that prioritizes saving the living over the dead.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 救生不救死者 - Reflects a moral principle common in Confucian thought, emphasizing the value of life.
  • 救官不救民者 - Reflects a societal hierarchy where officials are often given priority over common people.
  • 救大不救小者 - Reflects a hierarchical system where higher-ranking officials are given more leniency.
  • 救旧不救新者 - Reflects a preference for established officials over newcomers, possibly due to their experience and connections.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 救生不救死者，死者已死，断无可救，生者尚生，又杀以抵命，是多死一人也 - Emphasizes the moral dilemma of choosing between saving the living and the dead.
  • 宁委曲以出之，而死者衔冤与否，则非所计也 - Highlights the prioritization of saving the living over the dead, even if it means the dead may harbor a grudge.
  • 救官不救民者，上控之案，使冤得申，则官之祸福不可测，使不得申，即反坐，不过军流耳 - Outlines the potential outcomes of appealing cases and the varying degrees of punishment.
  • 救大不救小者，罪归上官，则权位重者谴愈重，且牵累必多，罪归微官，则责任轻者罚可轻，且归结较易 - Compares the consequences of attributing crimes to higher versus lower-ranking officials.
  • 救旧不救新者，旧官已去，有所未了，羁留之恐不能偿，新官方来，有所委卸，强抑之尚可以办，其新官之能堪与否，则非所计也 - Discusses the implications of detaining old officials versus new ones.

  • 贻害无穷 - Refers to causing endless harm, which is a serious criticism in Chinese culture.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence '诚出关后，念父避罪亡命，必潜踪于僻地，凡深山穷谷，险阻幽隐之处，无不物色' describes the father's journey and actions after leaving the pass, including his search for a place to hide.

  • The clause '一日，于马家城山中遇老父，哀其穷饿，呼与语，询得其故，为之感泣' describes a specific encounter where the carpenter meets the father and learns his story.
  • The clause '引至家，款以酒食' describes the carpenter's hospitality and the act of bringing the father home.
  • The clause '俄有梓人携具入，计其年与父相等，子诚心动，谛审其貌，与母所说略相似' describes the carpenter's realization of his age and appearance, leading to a moment of emotional recognition.
  • The clause '因牵裙泣涕，具述其父出亡年月，且缕述家世及戚党，冀其或是' describes the son's emotional reaction and detailed recounting of his father's story.
  • The clause '是人且骇且悲，似欲相认，而自疑在家未有子，子诚具陈始末，乃噭然相持哭' describes the carpenter's mixed emotions and eventual recognition of the father.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces the idea that while classical interpretations should be relied upon, other less authoritative views should be considered but not taken as definitive.
  • This clause explains that the *Book of Han* categorizes the birth of three sons in one birth as a human ailment or societal issue.
  • This rhetorical question contrasts the birth of four-breasted scholars in the Zhou dynasty with the birth of three sons in one birth, questioning why the former is seen as a sign of divine favor while the latter is seen as an omen.
  • This passage uses natural metaphors to argue that the birth of three sons in one birth is not solely due to the mother's qi but also involves the father's contribution.
  • This clause argues that if the birth of three sons in one birth were due to the mother alone, it could be seen as a human ailment, but since the father is involved, it is not solely due to the mother's excessive qi.
  • This rhetorical question extends the argument to other symbolic phenomena, questioning whether they too should be interpreted as signs of excessive natural forces.
  • This clause summarizes the author's view that the *Great Plan of the Five Elements* often contains forced interpretations, with this particular case being especially problematic.
  • This clause argues that the *Great Plan* should not be elevated to the status of a sacred text simply because it was transmitted by Fu Sheng.
  • This clause explains that the state has established a policy of rewarding the birth of three sons in one birth, effectively rejecting outdated and biased interpretations.
  • This clause explains the author's decision to revise the *Continued Comprehensive Examination* by removing the category of auspicious and inauspicious signs, in accordance with official decree.
  • This clause provides a personal note about the author's writing process and the inclusion of additional commentary.

  • 使功成事定之後，殁于战阵者，皆挟以为仇 - 如果那些在成功後戰場上死去的人，都懷恨在心，是一個假設的情況，暗示所有戰死的士兵都會尋仇。

  • 有是理乎 - 這合理嗎？是一種修辭問句，質疑前一說法的邏輯。

  • 何忽今日而为神，何乎今日而报怨 - 為什麼他今天突然變成神，為什麼今天要報仇，質疑顏良鬼魂現身的合理性。
  • 揆以天理，殆必不然 - 根據天理，這肯定不會發生，斷言前一情景的不可能性。
  • 是盖庙祝师巫，造为诡语，山妖水怪，因民所荧惑而依砶之 - 這可能是廟祝和巫師編造的奇異故事，山精水怪利用百姓的迷惑而依附其上，暗示靈異現象的真實原因。

  • 丹阳县有袁双庙，真第四子也 - 丹陽縣有袁雙廟，真第四子，介紹袁雙廟及其供奉的袁真第四子。
  • 真为桓宣武诛，便失所在 - 袁真被桓宣武殺死，袁雙就消失了，解釋袁雙消失的原因。
  • 太元中形见于丹阳，求立庙，未即就功 - 太元年間袁雙在丹陽現身，請求立廟，但未立即建成，描述袁雙的現身和請求。
  • 大有虎灾，被害之家辄梦双至，催功甚急 - 有許多老虎災害，受害的家庭常常夢見袁雙到來，催促建廟非常急切，解釋建廟的急迫性。
  • 百姓立祠，于是猛暴用息 - 百姓建立了祠堂，於是猛獸的暴行停止了，顯示祠堂的效果。
  • 常以二月晦，鼓舞祈祠，其日恒风雨 - 總是二月的最後一天，在祠堂前舉行祈禱儀式，那天總是風雨交加，描述與祠堂相關的儀式。
  • 至元嘉五年，设奠讫，村人邱都于庙后见一物，人面鼍身，葛巾，七孔端正而有酒气 - 到元嘉年間第五年，設奠完畢，村民邱都在廟後見到一個東西，人面鼉身，戴著葛巾，七個孔洞端正且有酒氣，描述一次超自然遭遇。
  • 未知为双之神，为是物凭也 - 不知道是袁雙的神靈，還是被物體附身，表達對遭遇性質的不確定。
  • 余谓来必风雨，其为水怪无疑 - 我認為如果來時有風雨，那一定是水怪無疑，表達對遭遇性質的個人看法。
  • 然则是事古有之矣 - 然而這些事情自古就有，總結類似現象在過去也存在。

  • 此去昌吉九十里，我驰一日至城下 - '此去昌吉九十里' means 'it is ninety li to Changji,' and '我驰一日至城下' means 'I can ride there in one day.'
  • 彼逸而我劳，彼坐守而我仰攻 - '彼逸而我劳' means 'they are rested while we are fatigued,' and '彼坐守而我仰攻' means 'they are defending while we are attacking from above.'
  • 非百余人所能办也 - Means 'it cannot be done by just over a hundred men.'
  • 且此去昌吉皆平原 - Means 'and the way to Changji is all flat land.'
  • 玛纳斯河虽稍阔，然处处策马可渡，无险可扼 - '玛纳斯河虽稍阔' means 'the Manas River is somewhat wide,' and '然处处策马可渡，无险可扼' means 'but it can be crossed at any point by horse, with no defensible terrain.'
  • 所可扼者，此山口一线路耳 - Means 'the only defensible point is this narrow pass.'
  • 贼得城必不株守，其势当即来 - '贼得城必不株守' means 'the rebels will not stay in the city,' and '其势当即来' means 'they will immediately come.'
  • 公莫如驻兵于此，借陡崖遮蔽，贼不知多寡，俟其至而扼险下击 - '公莫如驻兵于此' means 'you should station your troops here,' '借陡崖遮蔽' means 'using the steep cliffs for cover,' '贼不知多寡' means 'the rebels won't know how many troops you have,' and '俟其至而扼险下击' means 'wait for them to arrive and attack from a defensible position.'
  • 是反攻为守，反劳为逸，贼可破也 - Means 'this turns defense into offense, fatigue into rest, and the rebels can be defeated.'

  • 却怪玉盘承露冷 - Means 'But I wonder why the jade plate holding the dew is cold,' a metaphorical expression that may refer to a cold, detached state or a symbolic representation of purity.
  • 香山居士太关情 - Refers to Bai Juyi, the 'Fragrant Hill Recluse,' suggesting that he is too emotionally involved, contrasting with the earlier detachment.
  • 多年密友，脱略形骸 - Means 'Long-time close friends, who are free and unconstrained,' describing a relationship where formalities are ignored.
  • 互以虐谑为笑乐 - Means 'They take each other's teasing and jesting as amusement,' indicating a playful, humorous relationship.
  • 初无成见于其闻也 - Means 'They had no preconceived notions about each other,' suggesting a lack of bias or judgment.
  • 蒋文恪公时为总裁 - Means 'Jiang Wenke, at the time, was the president,' referring to Jiang's position of authority.
  • 诸君子跌宕风流 - Means 'The gentlemen are unrestrained and elegant,' describing their free-spirited and refined nature.
  • 古人嫌隙，多起于俳谐 - Means 'Ancient people often developed animosity from playful jokes,' warning against the dangers of humor.
  • 不如并此无之，更全交之道耳 - Means 'It would be better not to have this, to preserve the integrity of friendship,' suggesting that avoiding humor can maintain relationships.
  • 皆深佩其言 - Means 'All deeply admired his words,' indicating agreement with Jiang's advice.
  • 盖老成之所见远矣 - Means 'This shows the wisdom of the experienced,' praising Jiang's insight.
  • 录之以志少年绮语之过 - Means 'Record this to remember the mistakes of youth,' suggesting that the passage serves as a lesson.
  • 后来英俊慎勿效焉 - Means 'Later generations of talented people should not follow this example,' advising against repeating the mistakes.

  • 堂吏谓之拜榜 - Refers to the court officials calling this act 'bowing to the list.'

  • 座主何以拜门生哉 - Asks 'why would the chief examiner bow to his students?'

  • 盖放榜之日，当即以题名录进呈 - Means 'on the day the list is posted, the list of successful candidates should be presented immediately.'

  • 必拆卷唱一名，榜填一名，然后付以填榜之纸条，写录一名 - Describes the process of opening the examination papers, announcing one name, filling in the list, and then providing a slip of paper to write down the name.
  • 今纸条犹谓之录条 - Means 'now the slip of paper is still called a 'recording slip.'
  • 必拜而送之，犹拜摺之礼也 - Means 'one must bow and send it, just like the ritual of bowing to a memorial.'
  • 榜不放，录不出 - Means 'the list is not posted, and the recording slips are not issued.'
  • 录不成，榜不放 - Means 'the recording slips are not completed, and the list is not posted.'
  • 故录与榜必并陈于案，始拜 - Means 'therefore, the recording slips and the list must be placed together on the table before the bowing.'
  • 榜大录小 - Means 'the list is large, and the recording slips are small.'
  • 灯光晃耀之下，人见榜而不见录 - Means 'under the bright light, people see the list but not the recording slips.'
  • 故误认为拜榜也 - Means 'so it is mistakenly believed to be bowing to the list.'
  • 厥后或缮录未完，天已将晓 - Means 'later, sometimes the recording is not finished, and dawn is approaching.'
  • 或试官急于复，命先拜而行 - Means 'or the examiners are in a hurry to return, and they order the bowing to proceed first.'
  • 遂有拜时不陈录于案者 - Means 'so there are times when the recording slips are not placed on the table during the bowing.'
  • 久而视为固然 - Means 'over time, this becomes seen as a natural thing.'
  • 堂吏或因可无录而拜，遂竟不陈录 - Means 'the court officials may bow without the recording slips, so they no longer place them on the table.'
  • 又因录既不陈，可暂缓而追送 - Means 'and because the recording slips are not placed, they can be delayed and sent later.'
  • 遂至写榜竣后，无录可陈，而拜遂潜移于榜矣 - Means 'so after the list is written, there are no recording slips to place, and the bowing has quietly shifted to the list.'
  • 尝以问先师阿文勤公 - Means 'I once asked my former teacher, Mr. Aw Wenqin.'
  • 公述李文贞公之言如此 - Means 'he recounted what Mr. Li Wenzheng said.'
  • 文贞即公己丑座主也 - Means 'Mr. Li Wenzheng was the chief examiner of Mr. Aw in the year of Gengchou.'

[1220]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 翰林院堂不启中门，云启则掌院不利 - Superstitious belief that opening the central door of the Hall of the Hanlin Academy would bring misfortune to the president of the academy.
  • 癸已开四库全书馆，质郡王临视 - Refers to the opening of the Siku Quanshu in the year of Gui-Si (癸巳), with Prince Zhi (质郡王) overseeing the event.
  • 司事者启之，俄而掌院刘文正公，觉罗奉公相继逝 - After the door was opened, both President Liu Wenzheng Gong (刘文正公) and Prince Jueluo Fong Gong (觉罗奉公) died shortly thereafter, reinforcing the superstition.
  • 门前沙堤中，有土凝结成丸，傥或误碎必损翰林 - Belief that the soil on the sand embankment in front of the gate forms into balls, and if these balls are accidentally broken, it will harm the Hanlin scholars.
  • 癸未雨水冲激露其一，为儿童掷裂，吴云岩前辈旋殁 - In the year of Gui-Wei (癸未), rainwater washed away one of these balls, and a child broke it, leading to the death of the elder Wu Yunyan (吴云岩).
  • 原心亭之西南隅，翰林有父母者，不可设坐，坐则有刑碠 - Belief that in the southwest corner of Yuanxin Pavilion, Hanlin scholars with parents should not sit, as doing so would bring misfortune.
  • 陆耳山时为学士，毅然不信，竟丁外艰 - Lu Ershan (陆耳山), a scholar-official, refused to believe this superstition and later experienced the death of his father, fulfilling the prediction.
  • 至左角门久闭不启，启则司事者有谴谪，无人敢试，不知果验否也 - The left side gate has been closed for a long time, and if it is opened, the person in charge will face punishment. No one dares to test this, leaving its truth unknown.
  • 其余部院，亦各有禁忌 - Other departments also have their own taboos.
  • 如礼部甬道屏门，旧不加搭渡，搭渡以夹木二方，夹于门限，坡陀如桥状，使堂官乘车者，可从中入，以免于旁绕 - The screen door of the Li Department's corridor used to have no bridge, but now it has a wooden bridge that allows officials to drive their carriages directly through, avoiding the need to go around.
  • 钱箨石前辈不听，旋有天坛灯杆之事者 - Qian Tuoshi (钱箨石), an elder, ignored this practice, and shortly after, there was an incident involving the lamp pole at the Temple of Heaven, reinforcing the belief in the importance of following such practices.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 翰林院 - The Hanlin Academy, a prestigious institution in the imperial court responsible for literary and scholarly tasks.
  • 四库全书馆 - The Siku Quanshu (Complete Library in Four Sections) was a massive compilation of Chinese literature and scholarship during the Qing dynasty.
  • 质郡王 - Prince Zhi, a member of the imperial family who oversaw the opening of the Siku Quanshu.
  • 刘文正公 - President Liu Wenzheng Gong, a prominent figure in the Hanlin Academy.
  • 觉罗奉公 - Prince Jueluo Fong Gong, another high-ranking official.
  • 吴云岩 - An elder scholar, Wu Yunyan, who died after the ball was broken.
  • 陆耳山 - Lu Ershan, a scholar-official who disbelieved in the superstition and later experienced the death of his father.
  • 礼部 - The Ministry of Rites, one of the six ministries in the imperial government, responsible for rituals, ceremonies, and cultural affairs.
  • 天坛灯杆 - The lamp pole at the Temple of Heaven, a significant religious and ceremonial site.

  • 夜半有数盗执炬露刃入 - At midnight, several thieves entered with torches and exposed their weapons. '露刃' (lùrèn) is a vivid description of the thieves brandishing their weapons, emphasizing their threat.

  • 愿乞夫人一物 - I wish to ask for one thing from Madam. '愿乞' (yuànxī) is a polite and formal way of making a request, which adds a layer of politeness to the thieves' actions.
  • 愿乞夫人一物，夫人勿惊 - I wish to ask for one thing from Madam, Madam, please do not be alarmed. The repetition of '夫人' (fūrén) emphasizes the address to the woman, making it clear that the request is directed at her.
  • 即割一左耳 - They immediately cut off one of her left ears. '割' (gē) is a strong verb meaning 'to cut off' or 'to sever,' which adds to the intensity of the action.
  • 敷以药末 - They applied some medicine powder. '敷以' (fūyǐ) means 'to apply' or 'to spread,' and '药末' (yào mò) refers to powdered medicine, which suggests a specific treatment.
  • 数日勿洗，自结痂愈也 - Do not wash it for several days; it will naturally form a scab and heal. '自结痂愈' (zì jié jiā yù) is a medical term indicating that the wound will heal naturally without further intervention.
  • 遂相率呼啸去 - They then left, shouting and laughing. '呼啸' (hūxiào) conveys a sense of noise and laughter, suggesting that the thieves left in a boisterous manner.
  • 怖几失魂 - She was so terrified that she almost lost her soul. '怖几失魂' (bù jī shī hún) is a hyperbolic expression indicating extreme fear, which adds to the emotional impact of the story.
  • 以为仇耶？不杀不淫 - Was this an act of revenge? They did not kill or rape. '以为仇耶' (yǐ wéi chóu yé) is a rhetorical question, and '不杀不淫' (bù shā bù yín) emphasizes the thieves' restraint.
  • 以为盗耶？未劫一物 - Was this an act of theft? They did not take anything. '未劫一物' (wèi jié yī wù) is another rhetorical question, emphasizing that the thieves did not steal anything.
  • 既不劫不杀不淫矣，而又戕其耳 - Since they did not steal, kill, or rape, why did they cut off her ear? '戕其耳' (qiāng qí ěr) is a strong expression of the act of cutting off the ear, which adds to the mystery of the thieves' motives.
  • 既戕其耳矣，而又赠以良药 - Having cut off her ear, they also gave her good medicine. '赠以良药' (zèng yǐ liáng yào) indicates that the thieves provided a beneficial treatment, which adds a layer of complexity to their actions.
  • 是专为取耳来也？ - Was this specifically to take her ear? '专为取耳来也' (zhuān wéi qǔ ěr lái yě) is a rhetorical question, emphasizing the unusual nature of the act.
  • 取此耳又何意耶？ - What was the purpose of taking this ear? '取此耳又何意耶' (qǔ cǐ ěr yòu hé yì yé) is another rhetorical question, highlighting the mystery surrounding the thieves' motives.
  • 千思万索，终不得其所以然 - After much thought and consideration, I still cannot understand the reason. '千思万索' (qiān sī wàn suǒ) means 'to think and search in all directions,' indicating a thorough but unsuccessful attempt to understand the situation.
  • 天下真有理外事也 - Indeed, there are truly events beyond reason in the world. '理外事' (lǐ wài shì) refers to events that defy logical explanation, which adds a philosophical dimension to the story.
  • 邱生曰：苟得此盗，自必有其所以然，其所以然亦必在理中，但定非我所见之理耳 - Qiu said: If I could catch these thieves, I would certainly understand their motives. Their motives must also be within reason, but they are certainly not the kind of reason I can understand. '苟得此盗' (gǒu dé cǐ dào) means 'if I could catch these thieves,' and '其所以然亦必在理中' (qí suǒ yǐ rán yì bì zài lǐ zhōng) emphasizes that the motives are rational, even if they are not immediately apparent.
  • 然则论天下事，可据理以断有无哉！ - Therefore, when discussing matters of the world, can we rely on reason to determine their existence or non-existence? '据理以断有无' (jù lǐ yǐ duàn yǒu wú) means 'to judge existence or non-existence based on reason,' which raises a philosophical question about the limits of reason.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汝两曾伯祖 - Refers to the speaker's great-uncles, emphasizing family lineage and respect for ancestors.
  • 一讳景星，一讳景辰 - Names of the great-uncles, with '讳' (huì) indicating respect for their names.
  • 皆名诸生也 - Scholars or students in ancient China, reflecting the value placed on education.
  • 神荼郁垒 - Mythological figures believed to guard the gates of hell, often depicted on doorways for protection.
  • 尉迟敬德秦琼 - Historical figures, Wei Chi Jingde and Qin Qiong, who were famous generals and protectors in Chinese history.
  • 丘处机西游记 - A historical record by Qiu Chuji, a Taoist master, not to be confused with the famous novel *Journey to the West*.
  • 委巷小说 - Popular, less reputable literature, often dismissed by scholars.
  • 东方朔神异经 - An ancient text attributed to Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar and court jester during the Han dynasty.
  • 姚安公 - A title or name of a person, likely a respected figure in the speaker's family or community.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sentence introduces the two great-uncles and their status as scholars, setting the context for the story.
  • This clause describes the action of the great-uncles just before the incident, creating a vivid scene.
  • The debate centers on the identity of the figures depicted, with the great-uncles arguing against the old man's interpretation.
  • The old man's reaction and his attempt to prove his point using a historical record.
  • The great-uncles' response, dismissing the old man's evidence and bringing in a more authoritative text.
  • The prolonged debate and its consequences, leading to the great-uncles being trapped.
  • The next day's events, with the great-uncles about to leave but finding the city already surrounded.
  • The tragic outcome, with the city falling and the family meeting disaster.
  • The few survivors, emphasizing the loss and the importance of the remaining family members.
  • The critical moment and the great-uncles' focus on scholarly debate, highlighting their dedication to learning.

He washes it off but it happens again, gradually causing swelling, pain, and ulcers, eventually leading to blindness, describing the watchman's suffering.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 嘉庆丙辰冬 - The winter of 1806 during the reign of Emperor Jiaqing of the Qing dynasty.

[1252]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 后渐通猪语 - Means 'later, I gradually understood pig language.'

  • 躯干痴重，夏极苦热，惟汨没泥水中少可 - Means 'their bodies were heavy and hot in summer, and they could only find relief in the mud.'

  • 毛悚而劲，冬极苦寒，视犬羊软毳厚，有如仙兽 - Means 'their hair was stiff and prickly in winter, and they suffered greatly from the cold, while dogs and sheep with soft, thick fur seemed like divine beasts.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

[1253]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遇捕執時 - Means 'when captured and seized,' describing the moment of apprehension.
  • 姑跳踉奔避 - Literally 'temporarily leap and run to avoid,' indicating a desperate attempt to escape.
  • 冀緩須臾 - Means 'hoping to delay for a moment,' expressing the fleeting hope of escape.
  • 追得后蹴踏头项 - Literally 'after being caught, being kicked and stepped on the head and neck,' describing the brutal treatment after capture.
  • 拗捩蹄肘 - Means 'twisting and bending the hooves and elbows,' indicating physical torture.
  • 绳勒四足深至骨 - Literally 'ropes binding the four legs deeply to the bone,' describing severe restraint.
  • 痛若刀眘 - Means 'pain as if being cut by a knife,' emphasizing the intensity of the pain.
  • 或载以舟车 - Means 'or carried on boats and carts,' indicating transportation.
  • 重叠相压 - Literally 'stacked and pressed on each other,' describing the weight and pressure.
  • 肋如欲折 - Means 'ribs as if about to break,' indicating extreme physical strain.
  • 百脉涌塞 - Literally 'all veins blocked,' describing a state of physical distress.
  • 腹如欲裂 - Means 'abdomen as if about to split,' indicating severe abdominal pain.
  • 或贯以竿而扛之 - Means 'or carried on poles,' describing another method of transportation.
  • 更痛甚三木矣 - Literally 'more painful than the three wooden devices,' referring to a form of torture.
  • 至屠市提掷于地 - Means 'to the slaughterhouse, thrown to the ground,' describing the final destination and treatment.
  • 心脾皆震动欲碎 - Literally 'heart and spleen all shaken to the point of breaking,' indicating extreme emotional and physical distress.
  • 或即日死 - Means 'or die on the same day,' indicating the possibility of immediate death.
  • 或缚至数日 - Means 'or bound for several days,' indicating prolonged suffering.
  • 弥难忍受 - Means 'even more difficult to endure,' emphasizing the increasing difficulty of the suffering.
  • 时见刀俎在左 - Means 'often seeing the knife and cutting board to the left,' describing the presence of tools of slaughter.
  • 汤镬在右 - Refers to 'boiling pot to the right,' describing another tool of torture or execution.
  • 不知著我身时 - Means 'not knowing when it will happen to me,' indicating fear and uncertainty.
  • 辄簌簌战栗不止 - Means 'constantly trembling uncontrollably,' describing the physical reaction to fear.
  • 又时自顾己身 - Means 'also often looking at oneself,' indicating self-awareness.
  • 念将来不知磔裂分散 - Means 'thinking about the future, not knowing if it will be torn apart and scattered,' indicating fear of a gruesome fate.
  • 作谁家杯中羹 - Means 'become someone else's soup,' indicating a dehumanizing fate.
  • 凄惨欲绝 - Means 'extremely sad and pitiful,' describing the overall emotional state.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三木 - Refers to a form of torture involving wooden devices, often used in ancient Chinese punishments.
  • 刀俎 - Refers to the knife and cutting board, commonly used in butchery and symbolizing the act of slaughter.
  • 汤镬 - Refers to a boiling pot, often used in ancient Chinese executions by boiling alive.
  • 磔裂分散 - Refers to the act of being torn apart and scattered, a form of execution in ancient China.
  • 杯中羹 - Refers to soup, often used metaphorically to describe a dehumanizing fate where one's body is reduced to food.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sequence '遇捕執時，自知不免，姑跳踉奔避，冀缓须臾' describes the moment of capture and the desperate attempt to escape.
  • The sequence '追得后蹴踏头项，拗捩蹄肘，绳勒四足深至骨，痛若刀眘' describes the brutal treatment after capture.
  • The sequence '或载以舟车，则重叠相压，肋如欲折，百脉涌塞，腹如欲裂' describes the physical strain during transportation.
  • The sequence '或贯以竿而扛之，更痛甚三木矣' describes another method of transportation and the associated pain.
  • The sequence '至屠市提掷于地，心脾皆震动欲碎，或即日死，或缚至数日，弥难忍受' describes the final destination and the increasing difficulty of the suffering.
  • The sequence '时见刀俎在左，汤镬在右，不知著我身时，作何痛楚，辄簌簌战栗不止' describes the fear and uncertainty of the impending fate.
  • The sequence '又时自顾己身，念将来不知磔裂分散，作谁家杯中羹，凄惨欲绝' describes the self-awareness and fear of a gruesome fate.

[1258]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 凿然古经载籍所传 - Clear transmission by ancient scriptures and records, emphasizing the authenticity of the tradition.
  • 有凝思结念，终不一梦者 - Those who, despite deep thoughts and concentrated attention, never dream, highlighting the limitations of dreams.
  • 意识有时不能造 - The mind sometimes cannot create dreams, suggesting that dreams are not always within one's control.
  • 仓卒之患，意外之福有忽至而不知者 - Sudden misfortunes and unexpected blessings sometimes come suddenly without being known, emphasizing the unpredictability of life.
  • 气机有时不必感 - The qi (vital energy) sometimes does not respond, indicating that not all events are influenced by the flow of qi.
  • 天下之人如恒河沙数 - The people of the world are as numerous as the sands of the Ganges, using a metaphor to describe the vastness of the population.
  • 鬼神何独示梦于此人 - Why do spirits and deities show dreams only to this person, questioning the selective nature of divine communication.
  • 此人一生得失，亦必不一 - The gains and losses in this person's life are not one and the same, acknowledging the variability of life experiences.
  • 何独示梦于此事 - Why show dreams only about this matter, further questioning the specificity of the dream.
  • 事不可泄，何必示之 - If the matter is not to be revealed, why show it at all, raising the question of purpose in the dream.
